segments.s637-g57: هرانکس (a fused classical compound) = 'whoever/whosoever'; که is the relative/subordinating particle 'who/that' introducing the clause; بودند is the simple past plural 'they were' and places the existence/state in the past. The word order is subject (هرانکس که بودند) followed by the topical noun ایرانیان (the Iranians) which identifies which persons are meant.
segments.s637-g58: به رفتن ببستند is a classical periphrastic expression: ببستند (past, 3pl) here means 'they resolved / they concluded', and به رفتن is the verbal noun construction 'to go / for going' (i.e. they bound themselves to depart). با او میان = 'with him, among/in his company' (میان used adverbially = among/with). Overall this segment says they made the decision to go in his company.
segments.s637-g59: بیامد is a simple past 'he came'. چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. نزدیک دریا رسید literally 'reached near the sea' — نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near', دریا is the place 'sea', and رسید is past 'he reached/arrived'. The clause therefore reads 'when he drew near the sea, he arrived.'
segments.s637-g60: به ره is a prepositional phrase 'on/to the way' (locative: on the road). بار بازارگانان is a noun phrase 'the loads/cargo of the merchants' with بار (load/cargo) followed by its possessor بازارگانان. بدید is past 'he saw'. Word order places the locative first, then the possessed noun, then the verb: 'on the road he saw the merchants' loads.'
segments.s637-g61: که here introduces an explanatory or continuing clause ('for/that'). بازارگانان ایران places the possessor after the noun in a classical genitive-like order: 'merchants of Iran.' بدند is the past plural copula/verb 'they were.' Together: 'for there were merchants of Iran.'
segments.s637-g62: به آب و به خشکی is a paired locative expression 'on/at the water (sea) and on/at the land' (the repetition of به marks both places). دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave ones' and بدند again is past 'they were.' The clause states where the brave men were located: on sea and on land.
segments.s637-g63: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بازارگان is the subject 'the merchants' (plural). روی بهرام is the object phrase 'the face of Bahram' (classical juxtaposition of noun + name for genitive). دید is past 'they saw'. So: 'When the merchants saw Bahram's face...' ( subordinate temporal clause ).
segments.s637-g64: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor'. لب را uses the object marker را to mark 'the lip' as definite object. به دندان is an instrumental phrase 'with (his) tooth/teeth'. گزید is a past verb whose sense here is physical 'bit' (not 'choose'). The clause means the king bit his lip with his teeth.
segments.s637-g25: بهین زنان جهان is a nominal phrase 'the best of the women of the world' with the adjective بهین preceding the noun زنان and modified by the genitive جهان. آن بود is a past copular clause 'that (one) was,' where آن points back to the woman and بود is the past copula.
segments.s637-g26: This is a relative/result clause introduced by کزو 'from whom'; همواره خندان بود is the predicate 'was always cheerful/ smiling.' شوی here functions as a possessive/relational element meaning 'her husband' (poetic/archaic formation) rather than a verbal sense, so the provided lemma senses do not match.
segments.s637-g27: اگر introduces a conditional clause. پاک جانم is 'my pure soul' with the possessive suffix -م attached to جان (the suffix appears as a separate token here). ز پیمان تو is a prepositional phrase 'from/away from your pledge,' setting the thing the soul would turn away from.
segments.s637-g28: بپیچد is a subjunctive/irrealis verb 'were to turn aside / should turn.' به introduces the result clause and links to the consequent predicate بیزارم 'I would be averse/loath' (first‑person adjective with clitic). از جان تو marks the object/reason of that loathing: 'from/for life on your account.'
segments.s637-g29: بدو گفت بهرام shows inverted, poetic word order: indirect object (بدو 'to him') + verb گفت ('said') + subject بهرام. پس چاره کن is an imperative 'then find/do a remedy' where کن is the imperative auxiliary and چاره its object.
segments.s637-g30: وزین here is the conjunction+preposition (و + زین) introducing 'from/of this'; راز is 'secret.' مگشای is an older negative imperative 'do not open / do not disclose.' بر کس سخن literally 'speech to anyone' (بر marks the indirect object), so the phrase means 'speak it to no one.'
segments.s637-g31: سپینود گفت is the speech formula 'Sepīnūd said.' ای is the vocative 'O,' and سزاوار تخت is a descriptive vocative phrase 'worthy of the throne' (سزاوار governs تخت).
segments.s637-g32: بسازم is first‑person singular 'I will build/make.' اگر introduces the conditional clause; باشدم is an optative/subjunctive 'if I become / should I be' (here 'if I become'), and یار بخت is a fixed phrase 'ally/companion of Fortune' meaning 'if Fortune is on my side.'
segments.s637-g129: همی is a poetic adverb indicating continuous or habitual action ('kept on, was still'). راند is the simple past (3rd sing.) of راندن here meaning 'rowed/steered' or 'drove (the boat)'. تا introduces a limit 'until' and combines with the following locative phrase پیش دریا رسید, where پیش + noun means 'before/in front of' and رسید is the past 'reached/arrived'. Together: 'he kept rowing until he reached before the sea' → 'kept rowing until he reached the sea/shore.'
segments.s637-g130: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ایرانیان را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the Iranians' as the object of دید. همه 'all' modifies ایرانیان. خفته is an adjective/predicate 'asleep'. دید is the simple past 'saw'. The clause literal order (object before verb with را) is normal for Persian: 'when he saw the Iranians all asleep.'
segments.s637-g131: برانگیخت is a past (3rd sing.) verb meaning 'he roused/awakened' (here likely 'he roused them' or 'he raised [a force]'). کشتی و زورق are direct objects 'ships and small boats' connected by و. بساخت in classical usage functions as a past verb 'built/made', but the provided sense list does not include that meaning, so here it is being used as the past verb 'he made/built'. Word order places objects before the verb in a compact poetic style.
segments.s637-g132: به introduces direction 'into/to' (here 'into a boat'). زورق is 'boat'. سپینود is a proper name (Sepinud) and را is the object marker attached to it. در is the preposition 'in/inside'. نشاخت is an archaic/polite past-form verb meaning 'placed/settled/put (into)', but that specific sense is not listed in the provided senses, so it is marked as unknown here. The phrase reads 'he put Sepinud into the boat.'
segments.s637-g133: به خشکی رسیدند is a plural past 'they reached the dry land/shore' — به + locative + رسیدند (3rd pl. past 'arrived'). چون here means 'when'. روز گشت is a compressed phrase 'it became day' (روز = day; گشت = became), i.e. 'when day came/broke.'
segments.s637-g134: جهان‌پهلوان گیتی‌افروز is a two-part epithet/titles stacked together ('world‑champion, world‑illuminator'). گشت is the 3rd sing. past of گشتن used copulatively here 'became/appeared/rose'. The structure presents the hero with grand epithets followed by گشت to mean that the world‑champion, the world's illuminator, now manifested or came forth.
segments.s637-g81: بدیشان (to them) is a prepositional/dative phrase placed before the verb; چنین (thus) is an adverb modifying the speech; گفت is the past simple verb 'said' and appears before its subject (an inversion common in classical Persian); پس شهریار places the temporal adverb پس ('then') immediately before the subject شهریار ('the king').
segments.s637-g82: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what was said; نزد شما is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with you / in your keeping' (نزد = 'in the presence of / with'); از من marks origin/possession 'from me'; این زنهار is the noun phrase 'this warning' with این as the proximal determiner and زنهار the noun 'warning/admonition'. The whole clause specifies that the warning is from the speaker and should be kept by the addressees.
segments.s637-g83: بدارید is an imperative (2nd person plural) from داشتن used here as 'keep/hold'; و simply coordinates the two commands; با جان برابر کنید is an instruction 'make (it) equal to your life' where با جان ('with/against life') is the standard expression, برابر is 'equal', and کنید is the causative/imperative form of کردن 'do/make' acting on the implied object (the warning mentioned before).
segments.s637-g84: چو خواهید is a temporal/conditional phrase 'when/if you wish' (خواهید here = you wish/will). کز is a contracted form of 'که از' meaning 'that/from which' and introduces the subordinate clause; پندم 'my counsel' is the object marked by -م (my); افسر کنید is a compound verb phrase literally 'make (it) a helmet' (افسر = helmet) where کنید is again 'make/do' in the imperative/optative sense. The clause means 'make my advice into your helmet (i.e. your protection).'
segments.s637-g85: گر is the conditional 'if'; از من شود literally 'if it happens from/because of me' (از + من indicates cause/agent here); تخت پرداخته is a noun plus past-participle/adjectival form—تخت 'throne' modified by پرداخته (a past participle) describing the throne's altered state. شود is the present/future/conditional form 'becomes/should be' governing the whole conditional clause.
segments.s637-g86: سپاه آید is a simple nominal + verb clause 'an army/host comes' (آید here = will come/comes). از هر سوی ساخته follows as a circumstantial phrase 'assembled/made from every side' where از هر سوی = 'from every direction' and ساخته is a past participle/adjectival 'made/assembled'. Together the clause predicts that troops will arrive from all sides.
segments.s637-g87: نه ... ماند ایدر ... نه introduces a correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. بازارگان ماند ایدر means 'no merchants remain here' (ماند is the past/present-stative 'remain' and ایدر = 'here'); the following نه شاه continues the negative list 'nor a king.' The word order places the negation and first item before the verb, which is common in classical Persian listing.
segments.s637-g88: The negative list continues: نه دهقان نه لشکر نه تخت و کلاه 'neither landowners/peasants, nor army, nor throne and hat/crown.' Each noun is linked by repeated نه (neither/nor), and تخت و کلاه ('throne and hat') is a fixed pairing meaning sovereignty/regalia. The verb ماند from the previous segment governs all items (no one remains).
segments.s637-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. The clause follows a subject + prepositional phrase + verb order: بهرام (subject) با دخت شنگل (prepositional phrase 'with the maiden Shangal') and then the past tense verb بساخت (an older/poetic past of ساختن meaning 'took/matched/to wed' here). The whole clause means 'When Bahram took/was joined with the maiden Shangal.'
segments.s637-g2: This is a main clause where زن او ('his wife' / 'the woman of him') is the subject and شناخت is the past action 'knew'/'recognized'. همی is an emphatic/stative particle in classical Persian often strengthening the verb (here: 'indeed' / 'so'), and شاه گیتی is a title phrase 'king of the world' that functions as the object/complement of شناخت: 'his woman indeed knew (recognized) [him] as king of the world.'
segments.s637-g3: This clause lists time (شب و روز, 'night and day') followed by the participial/adjectival گریان 'weeping' describing the subject. بد functions adverbially here 'badly/sorely,' and از + مهر اوی gives the cause 'because of / from his love of her' (اوی is the enclitic possessive 'his/her' referring back to the beloved). The overall sense: 'Night and day he was weeping sorely because of his love for her.'
segments.s637-g4: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed/put' and takes its object immediately: دو چشم 'two eyes'. اندران is a classical locative/relational compound meaning 'in that / therein'; چهر اوی = 'that face of hers' (اوی = her). So literally 'he had placed two eyes in that face of hers' — a poetic way to say tears filled her eyes or that two eyes (i.e. tearful eyes) sat on her face.
segments.s637-g5: چو 'when' introduces the subordinate temporal clause. از مهرشان 'of/from their love' gives the content that caused awareness; مهرشان is 'their/one another's love' (شان = their). شنگل is the subject and آگاه شد is the compound past 'became aware / learned' (آگاه + شد).
segments.s637-g6: ز (poetic for 'from' / 'because of') introduces the cause or source; بدها 'bad things/evil thoughts' is the plural noun, گمانیش 'his suspicion/thought' (گمان + enclitic یِش = his) is the thing affected, کوتاه شد is a passive-like phrasing 'was made short / was cut short' (شد as auxiliary indicating change of state). Together: 'From the bad things his suspicion was shortened' = 'his ill suspicions were dispelled.'
segments.s637-g7: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat' (classical narrative past). The phrase یک روز شادان 'one joyful day' is a temporal adverbial, and بهم 'together/with one another' marks the mutual gathering: 'They sat together on a joyful day.'
segments.s637-g8: همی رفت is an archaic/progressive expression meaning 'went on / passed' (همی adding iterative/progressive aspect). هرگونه 'every kind / all manner' is the object of that verbal idea, and از بیش و کم 'of more and less' is an idiom meaning 'of great and small things' or 'everything, matters large and small'. So the clause says they passed the time speaking of all sorts of things.
segments.s637-g113: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (here introducing a subordinate time clause). برساخت is a past action 'rose/stood up' (classical poetic past of برساختن) and takes the subject شنگل (name) that follows; که introduces the following subordinate purpose/location clause 'that he comes/goes', and آید (present/subjunctive form of آمدن) means 'comes/goes'. به + دشت marks direction 'into/to the plain'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal conjunction + verb (برساخت) + subject (شنگل) + relative/conjunctive که + verb (آید) + prepositional phrase (به دشت).
segments.s637-g114: زنش is the possessed noun 'his wife' (زن + enclitic possessive ش). گفت is the verb 'said' (past of گفتن) introducing direct speech; the following phrase reports what she said: برزوی is used here as a proper name (Barzū) and بیمار گشت is a two-word predicate 'became sick' (بیمار 'sick' + گشت past of گشتن 'turned/become'). The sentence mixes narrative past (گفت, گشت) to report speech and a change of state.
segments.s637-g115: به پوزش is a prepositional phrase 'with a request for pardon / by way of apology' (به + پوزش). همی is an emphatic/progressive particle often used in classical Persian to indicate ongoing or customary action; together همی گوید would mean 'she says / she kept saying'. گوید here is the speech verb 'says' (classical present), and ای شهریار is the direct address 'O king'.
segments.s637-g116: تو is the vocative/pronoun 'you' (addressing the king). دل را is the definite/direct-object-marked noun 'the heart' (دل + را marking the theme). بمن means 'toward me / on my account'. هیچ here intensifies the negation 'not at all / in any way'. رنجه is the verb (infinitive-root form used imperatively/negatively here) meaning 'to be troubled/distressed' and مدار is a negative imperative 'do not do' (classical poetic prohibitive: مدار = مَ + دار/an older negative command form), so the phrase literally commands 'Do not at all trouble your heart for me.'
segments.s637-g117: چو again introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. ناتندرستی is an abstract noun 'ill health / sickness'. بود is the past/present copula 'was/was present' (classical auxiliary 'there was / there is'), and جشنگاه is the noun 'place/occasion of festivity'. Together they state a general observation 'when illness is present, the festal place [is affected]'.
segments.s637-g118: دژم باشد is a predicative clause meaning 'is/turns sullen or grim' (دژم 'sullen' + باشد, a classical subjunctive/present form of بودن used here as 'is/would be'). و is the conjunction 'and'. داند is the present/past classical form of 'to know' (here 'knows' or 'is aware'), این مایه = 'this cause/reason', and شاه 'the king' is the subject of that verb; word order puts the known item before شاه for emphasis: 'and the king knows this reason.'
segments.s637-g119: به زن گفت شنگل is the frame 'Shangal said to the woman/wife' (به + زن marking the addressee; گفت = 'said'; شنگل = subject). که این خود مباد is a classical optative/negative formula: که introduces the wished-for clause, این خود 'this itself' (an emphatic demonstrative), and مباد is an old optative/subjunctive meaning 'may it not be' (a classical negative wish). The whole segment is Shangal's prefatory clause meaning 'may this not be the case.'
segments.s637-g120: که introduces the content of the wish 'that ...'. بیمار باشد is a subjunctive-like clause 'be ill' (بیمار 'sick' + باشد as a present/subjunctive of بودن: 'be'), and کند is the verb 'makes/does' (here causative/active 'turns into / makes'), followed by جشن یاد, a collocation 'jashn-yād' where جشن 'feast, celebration' and یاد 'remembrance' together imply 'a celebration turned into a mere remembrance/commemoration.' The clause thus expresses the fear 'that, because of illness, the festival should be reduced to mere remembrance.'
segments.s637-g121: ز + place introduces origin ('from Kannauj'). شبگیر is a postpositive epithet meaning 'night-roving/night-visiting' modifying شنگل. برفت is an archaic/poetic past tense (from رفتن with the prefix بر-) meaning 'departed/went away' and completes the clause.
segments.s637-g122: This clause describes Shangal's action toward the Indians. ابا here is used as a verb with the sense of reluctance/aversion (classical sense 'to be unwilling/averse'); هندوان is the object 'the Indians'; روی (here 'face') + بنهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed/turned') form a light-verb construction meaning 'he turned/put his face' and تفت (a verbal/noun meaning 'scorching/heat') qualifies that face — i.e. 'he fixed a scorching look.'
segments.s637-g123: چو ... شد is a temporal clause ('when ... became'); شب تیره شد = 'the night became dark.' شاه بهرام is the nominative apposition 'King Bahram.' گفت is the report verb 'said' (classical past narration). Word order follows subject/title then the verb of saying.
segments.s637-g124: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that it is the case that'). آمد گه = 'the time has come' (آمد + گه 'time/occasion'). رفتن‌ای is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to go' with the vocative particle ای attached to address the addressees; نیک جفت is a vocative phrase 'O good pair' (نیک = good/auspicious, جفت = pair/spouses). The clause is direct reported speech ordering: 'that has come the occasion of going, O good pair.'
segments.s637-g125: بیامد is a simple past 'came.' سپینود is the personal name acting as subject; را is the definite object marker attached to the following noun phrase (marks the thing being acted upon). برنشاند is a past transitive verb (بر + نشاندن) meaning 'set/placed/caused to sit' — together: 'Sepinud came and seated him.'
segments.s637-g126: همی is a frequentive/continuous particle here meaning roughly 'then/so' or 'straightaway'; پهلوی means 'beside/at the side of'; نام یزدان is the noun phrase 'the name of God'; بخواند is a presentive/past-tense recitative verb (here deponent in narrative) meaning 'he recited/said' — so the clause means 'then he recited the name of God at his side.'
segments.s637-g127: بپوشید is a past tense verb 'he covered/put a cover over'; خفتان (here a nominalized plural) 'the sleepers' is the object of پوشید; و coordinates two actions ('and'); خود emphasizes the subject ('he himself'); برنشست is a past-action verb 'sat up / took his seat' — so 'he covered the sleepers and he himself sat up.'
segments.s637-g128: This is a verbless noun phrase listing his equipment. کمندی ('a lariat/lasso') + به فتراک ('at/on the saddle-cloth' — به marking location or attachment) and گرزی ('a mace') + به دست ('in the hand' with به marking instrumentality/location). The nouns are placed in a juxtaposed list to describe what he had ready.
segments.s637-g49: ز is the brief poetic preposition meaning 'from' / 'because of' and introduces the cause ('because of his words'). گفتار او is a Genitive phrase 'his speech/words' (گفتار = speech, او = his). گشت is the past of گشتن here used as 'became' (intransitive change of state) and takes the following predicate complement شاد 'happy' with the subject بهرام (proper name) placed before the adjective.
segments.s637-g50: نخفت is negative past: ن‑ prefix + خفت (past of خوابیدن), so 'he did not sleep.' اندر اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'in/within thought' (اندر = in; اندیشه = thought). تا بامداد is a temporal clause 'until dawn/morning' (تا = until; بامداد = dawn). The three parts together state the duration/result of his wakefulness.
segments.s637-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بنمود is the past of نمودن/نمودن used as 'appeared/shone' (classical past). خورشید is the subject 'the sun.' بر + چرخ is a locative phrase 'upon the wheel/sky' (چرخ = the sky/heaven, here poetic). دست follows as an image 'hand' (literally 'the hand upon the sky'), a pictorial phrase; the whole line is poetic word order describing the sun's appearance.
segments.s637-g52: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'the dark night' (تیره = dark/ominous). بار is used poetically as 'burden/weight' (here the weight/covering of night). غریبان is the plural 'strangers/foreigners' and is the indirect object/affected group. ببست is the past of بستن 'he closed/shut/bound' with شب as subject: 'the dark night closed/laid its burden over the strangers.'
segments.s637-g53: نشست is the past of نشستن 'he sat / alit.' The classical combination از بر + noun often means 'from upon' (i.e., dismounting from something); از = from, بر = on/upon. باره here means 'load/baggage' (i.e., the pack or baggage). بهرام گور is the proper name 'Bahram Gūr' (گور as epithet 'Gūr/onager' attached to the name). The whole segment says he got down/off from Bahram Gūr's baggage/mount.
segments.s637-g54: همی is an adverb of continuity 'kept on / continuously.' راند is past of راندن 'he drove/urged (on).' با ساز is instrumental 'with a tune/instrument' (با = with; ساز = tune/instrument — here 'tune'). نخچیر in hunting contexts is connected to 'game/prey' or hunting; here it qualifies the tune as a hunting tune. The final گور is the animal 'onager' (گور) — 'he drove the onager on to the sound of a hunting tune.'
segments.s637-g55: به زن گفت is the verb phrase 'he said to the woman' (به = to; زن = the woman; گفت = said — classical past of گفتن). بر ساز is an instruction 'on (the) instrument / play the tune' (بر here marks the instrument/medium). و با کس مگوی is an imperative negative addressed to her: و = 'and'; با کس = 'to anyone/with anyone'; مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not tell' (م‑ negative + گو = tell).
segments.s637-g56: نهادیم is the perfect/past 1st‑person‑plural of نهادن 'we placed/put / we set' used narratively as 'we set out' or 'we set in motion.' هر دو = 'both/two of them.' سوی = 'toward(s)'; راه = 'the road/way'; روی in this collocation functions as a locative/adverbial 'upon/along' (راه روی = 'on the road' / 'toward the road'). The segment says they/they two proceeded, each toward the road (i.e., they set off along the road).
segments.s637-g97: چو is the poetic conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (here referring to the king). شاه is the subject 'the king'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' referring back to what was heard. گرفت آفرین is a light verbal idiom: گرفت (literally 'took') + the noun آفرین = 'took up/uttered the acclamation', i.e. 'he cried “Afarin!”'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal marker + verb + subject + object phrase + subsequent verbal idiom.
segments.s637-g98: بران here functions as a prepositional/colocational form ('بر' + 'آن') meaning 'at/to those' or 'upon those.' نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/illustrious ones.' با فر و دین is a descriptive phrase 'with farr and dīn' (atributing the qualities of royal glory and religion/obligation); together the phrase specifies to whom the king's acclamation was directed.
segments.s637-g99: همی is the frequentative/continuative particle used in epic poetry meaning 'still/kept on.' رفت is the simple past 'went' (here past narrative). پیچان is a participial/adverbial adjective 'winding/turning' modifying the motion. به ایوان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to his iwan' — خویش is expressed with the possessive clitic ش on خوی ('his own'). Word order places the manner (پیچان) before the destination.
segments.s637-g100: به introduces the indirect object 'to God' (یزدان). سپرده is a past participle/predicate meaning 'entrusted' (i.e. 'having entrusted'). تن و جان خویش = 'his body and soul' where خویش + enclitic ش expresses possession. The clause places the entrustment (به یزدان سپرده) before its objects for poetic order: 'entrusted to God (his) body and soul.'
segments.s637-g101: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'thereupon' or 'at that moment.' که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when.' بهرام is the subject (proper name). شد is an inchoative/past auxiliary here meaning 'moved/turned' or 'set out.' سوی راه = 'toward the road.' The sequence denotes a temporal action: at that moment Bahram started for the road.
segments.s637-g102: چنین گفت is the narrative formula 'thus he said.' با زن = 'to/with the woman' (i.e. he spoke to the woman). که‌ای before a vocative is an archaic/classical particle forming the address; here it functions like 'که ای' = 'O'. نیک‌خواه is a vocative noun 'well-wisher' or 'kind-hearted one.' Altogether: 'he said to the woman: “O well-wisher.”' Note: classical diction places the formulaic thus (چنین) before گفت.
segments.s637-g103: ابا is an imperative/exhortation sense 'do not be reluctant / do not refuse' (classical usage). مادر خویشتن = 'mother yourself' or 'O mother, (of yourself)' with خویشتن 'one's self' (emphatic/reflexive). چاره‌ساز literally 'make a remedy' or 'be the solver' — used here as an imperative/advice 'devise a plan / contrive a remedy.' The clause is an exhortation addressed to the mother (second person).
segments.s637-g104: چنان کو begins a relative clause 'such that / one who.' کو here is the classical relative 'who/that.' درستی is 'uprightness / truth / rightness' (an abstract noun). نداندت is a form meaning 'does not make you know / does not reveal to you' (the -ت is the 2nd-person object clitic 'you'). راز 'secret' is the thing not revealed. Grammatically the clause explains the situation: 'you are such that even rightness/truth will not disclose the secret to you.'
segments.s637-g17: This clause is inverted in typical Persian order: به ایران (for Iran) marks the beneficiary; مرا را (here split as م + را) stands for 'my' / 'to me' (classical object/possessive clitic), followed by کار (work/deed) as the subject/topic. زین بهترست = 'is better than this' (زین = 'than this' in older usage, here fused into the phrase). The copular sense 'is better' is carried by بهترست and evaluates the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s637-g18: This is a short nominal clause: همم (one's aim/effort) is the subject, followed by the title کردگار جهان (the Maker of the world, i.e. God) as nominative/predicate complement; یاورست is the predicative verb meaning 'is a helper' (the -ست ending is the present/certain copula in classical style). Together: 'my aim/effort — the world's Creator is my helper.'
segments.s637-g19: به رفتن‌گر is an older conditional/participial construction: رفتن‌گر = 'one who goes / if you go'; به here signals the act/aim ('in going' / 'if you go'). ایدونک is a small adverbial/particle adding immediacy or sequence, and رای آیدت = 'a plan/counsel comes to you' (رای 'counsel, plan'; آیدت 'comes to you' with the enclitic -ت). The clause expresses consequence on the condition of going.
segments.s637-g20: Parallel consequence clause: به خوبی خرد رهنمای آیدت = 'a guide of good wisdom comes to you.' به marks the benefactive/experiential, خوبی خرد is a genitive-like sequence 'goodness (or goodness-of) wisdom' modifying رهنمای (guide/adviser), and آیدت again 'comes to you.' Word order places modifiers before the head noun (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s637-g21: به هر جای = 'in every place'; نام تو = 'your name' (possessive pronoun follows the noun in classical Persian); بانو بود = 'was (a) lady' (بانو as predicate noun, بود past copula). The clause states that in each place her name/title was known as 'Banu.'
segments.s637-g22: This clause gives a concrete image: پدر پیش تختت = 'your father before your throne' (پیش = 'in the presence of/before'), به زانو بود = 'was on his knees' / 'knelt' (به + زانو marks posture; بود is past copula). Word order places the location/scene before the verb, as common in narrative.
segments.s637-g23: سپینود is the speaker's name. گفت here introduces direct speech ('said'); ای is the vocative particle 'O', and سرافراز مرد is the address 'exalted/proud man' (adjective before noun). This is a conventional narrative tag introducing the quoted admonition.
segments.s637-g24: This is the admonition itself. تو (you) is followed by an imperative or prohibition expressed elliptically: بر خیره (lit. 'upon astonishment') + از راه دانش مگرد = 'do not be astonished; do not turn aside from the way of knowledge.' مگرد is an old negative imperative 'do not turn/withdraw' (here used with از راه دانش 'from the way of wisdom'). Word order places the object/reason phrase after the verb cluster in poetic style.
segments.s637-g33: یکی here functions as an indefinite/existential 'a certain' (literary 'there is a...'). جشنگاهست is the compound of جشنگاه + است (written together in the manuscript), meaning 'festival‑place' or 'place of celebration'. ز + ایدر نه دور is a poetic locative phrase: ز = 'from' (poetic), ایدر = 'here', and نه دور = 'not far' (negation before the adjective); together 'not far from here'.
segments.s637-g34: که introduces a relative clause ('where/which'). سازد is 3rd‑person singular present ('makes/sets up' or 'holds'), agreeing with the subject. پدرم is 'my father' with the enclitic -م (possessive); اندران = اندر + ان = 'therein/inside that' (locative), بیشه = 'grove', سور = 'feast/banquet' (the object of سازد). Word order places locative (اندران بیشه) before the noun سور in this poetic line.
segments.s637-g35: که again links this clause to the previous noun ('which'). دارند is 3rd‑person plural present 'they have/possess'. فرخ مران likely functions as a (compound) proper name or epithet + name ('the fortunate Maran' or 'Ferokh‑Maran'); in classical poetic order the verb may precede the subject. جای را is the definite object with را marking the place as the object possessed.
segments.s637-g36: ستایند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they praise/they honor'. جای بت‌آرای را is the object: جای 'place', modified by بت‑آرای (an adjective/compound meaning 'idol‑adorned' or 'adorned for idols'), with را again marking the definite object. The verb precedes the object here, a common poetic order.
segments.s637-g37: بود is the copula/existential 'it was' (narrative past/present sense). تا here means 'as far as' or 'up to' (distance). بران = بر + آن ('to/upon that') points to the previously mentioned grove. فرسنگ بیست gives the measure: 'twenty farsangs/parasang' — the order is inverted for poetic diction (normally بیست فرسنگ).
segments.s637-g38: که introduces the clause describing that place ('where'). پیش with بت indicates 'in the presence of / before the idol' (پیش = 'before, in front of, in the presence of'). اندر = 'in/within' is a locative particle; بباید + گریست is an impersonal/deliberative construction: بباید = 'it is necessary/one must' and گریست (infinitive/masdar‑like form) = 'to weep/lament', so 'one must lament before the idol.'
segments.s637-g39: بدان is the fused locative demonstrative (به + آن) meaning 'at/in that'. جای = 'place'. نخچیر گوران is a genitive construction: نخچیر 'game, prey, or spoil' modified by گوران ('the Guran' — here likely a tribal or group name), i.e. 'the Guran's game/spoils'. بود is the copula 'there was'. Word order places the locative demonstrative first for emphasis.
segments.s637-g40: به = 'at/to/for' introducing the following location; قنوج is the place name Kannauj. در can be 'in/inside' or 'the gate/door' here best read 'in/inside (the gate)'. عود سوزان is a noun + participial adjective: عود 'agarwood / oud' and سوزان 'burning' (literally 'burning agarwood'). بود again is the copula 'there was' (or 'it burned' in sense of 'there was... burning').
segments.s637-g65: نفرمود is the past negative of فرمود and here means 'he did not command/say'; it governs the following infinitive بردن ('to bring/to take') as its object (common classical structure: verb of saying/command + infinitive). به پیشش places the action 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + enclitic -ش), and نماز is the thing to be brought (here 'prayer' / 'petitioner' in the sense of a supplication). The whole clause reads: 'he did not order the bringing of the prayer/petitioner before him.'
segments.s637-g66: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/away from'; نادان is the group being excluded ('the ignorant'). سخن را is the definite object/topic ('the talk' marked by را), and همی داشت راز is literal 'kept (داشت) the talk a secret (راز)' with همی adding an emphatic/continuative flavor. Together: 'he kept the matter secret from the ignorant.'
segments.s637-g67: به بازارگان is the recipient phrase 'to the merchant' (به + noun). گفت is the past verb 'he said' used to introduce the direct command (note: the provided sense list for گفت did not include 'said', so the exact sense index is unavailable). لب را is the definite object 'the lip' (marked by را), and ببند is the imperative 'shut/close' directed at the merchant. The sequence is: 'he said to the merchant: "close your lips."'
segments.s637-g68: کزین = ک+از+این ('from/for this') is a poetic causal phrase 'for this reason'; سودمندی is 'usefulness/advantage'. و هم is 'and also', and با گزند literally 'with harm' (گزند = hurt/damage). The clause gives the reason for the injunction: 'for from this there is both benefit and harm.'
segments.s637-g69: گرین is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'گر این' meaning 'if this' (the sense lists given do not include this contraction, so index is -1). راز is the subject 'secret'; در هند is the locative 'in India'; پیدا شود is a passive/resultative phrase 'become found / be discovered' (شود is the subjunctive/optative 'become'). Together: 'If this secret is discovered in India...'.
segments.s637-g70: ز is the poetic 'from/because of'; خون خاک ایران is a genitive chain 'the blood of the soil of Iran' (i.e. Iran's soil soaked with blood). چو here means 'like/as', and دریا شود is 'becomes a sea' (شود = become). The clause paints the consequence: 'from blood the soil of Iran will be like a sea.'
segments.s637-g71: گشاده at the start is an instruction/adjective 'open/generous'. بران is poetic for 'برای آن' or 'برای آنِ' meaning 'for that' (i.e. 'toward that/for the one'), and کار is 'deed/case'. کو is an archaic relative (roughly 'که' = 'who/which') linking to لب ببست 'he closed his lip' (ببست = closed, past). The clause literally: 'Be open/generous toward that case/person which has closed his lips.'
segments.s637-g72: زبان‌بسته is a compound adjective 'tongue‑bound / tight‑lipped' (silent). باید is the modal 'must/ought to', and گشاده دو دست literally 'open two hands' (an idiom meaning 'open‑handed, generous'). Syntactically the modal governs the predicate 'گشاده دو دست': 'a tight‑lipped person must be met with open hands' (i.e. reward silence with generosity).
segments.s637-g89: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (poetic equivalent of وقتی). زان‌گونه (= ز + آن‌گونه) qualifies the following noun-phrase and means 'in that way / of that sort'; poetic word order places it before the verb. دیدند is simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they saw'. گفتار اوی is a genitive phrase 'his speech/words' where اوی is an archaic possessive pronoun 'his' placed after the noun.
segments.s637-g90: برفتند is past tense 3rd-person plural 'they departed/went away'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'at once / one and all' modifying the verb phrase (poetic placement after the verb). پر از آب روی is a predicative nominal phrase: پر از = 'full of', آب = 'water', روی = 'face' — the possessive is supplied by the subject 'they' (their faces were full of water).
segments.s637-g91: که introduces the content/wish (a clause giving what they say). جان بزرگان = 'the lives (جان) of the great (بزرگان)'. فدای تو is a predicate meaning 'sacrificed for you' (فدای with a following object/pronoun تو). باد here is the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic subjunctive), giving the sentence the sense 'may X be so'.
segments.s637-g92: جوانی و شاهی is a coordinated noun phrase 'youth and kingship'. روای is adjectival/ predicative from ر‍وا meaning 'fitting / appropriate'; syntactically it governs تو (to you) though the ezāfe is omitted as is common in poetic register. باد again is the optative 'may', so the whole means 'may youth and kingship be fitting to you.'
segments.s637-g93: اگر is the conditional 'if'. هیچ here functions in the sense 'ever / any' (i.e. 'if ever any ...'); راز تو = 'a secret of yours' with تو as the possessor. پیدا شود is a passive/colloquial-form verb phrase: پیدا (discovered) + شود (become / subjunctive) — together 'becomes discovered / is revealed' (future/conditional sense).
segments.s637-g94: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from / out of'. خون کشور ما = 'the blood of our country' (خون subject, کشور ما as genitive). چو دریا شود is a simile clause 'will become like the sea' (چو = like/as, دریا = sea, شود = 'will become' — third-person singular future/optative sense). The clause describes a catastrophic flooding of blood.
segments.s637-g95: که introduces the subordinate clause reporting thought. یارد is a personal noun 'the companion / friend' (here a person). بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' — in classical/poetic spelling the prefix ب (here written as ب) attaches to دین/این to make بدین = 'به این' ('in this'), and گونه = 'manner'. اندیشه کرد is past tense 'he thought' (اندیشه = thought, کرد = did/made — کامپوند فعل 'to think' formed with کردن).
segments.s637-g96: مگر here introduces a tentative/hypothetical sense ('perhaps' / 'maybe') leading into what he thought; بخت را is 'to Fortune/Fate' marked by را (object/ topical marker). گوید is the reported-speech verb 'he says/would say' (classical present/subjunctive sense introducing the quoted/implied command). از ره بگرد is the subordinate verbal phrase 'turn aside from the road' (از = 'from', ره = 'way/road', بگرد = imperative/subjunctive 'turn!' used as the action requested of Fortune).
segments.s637-g73: A noun phrase functioning as the direct object plus an instrumental phrase: 'زبان شما را' places the (definite) object 'your tongue/speech' before the verb using the object marker را. 'به سوگند سخت' is a prepositional/instrumental phrase ('by/with a strong oath'), where سخت qualifies سوگند (here ‘strong/solemn’). Word order (object before verb) is normal for Persian.
segments.s637-g74: ببندیم is a first-person-plural present/subjunctive (optative/jussive) form meaning 'let us bind' or 'we shall bind' (the imperfective prefix ب- plus ending -یم). تا introduces purpose ('so that') and takes بازیابیم in the subjunctive sense 'may we recover/let us recover'; بخت is the goal 'fortune/lot'. The clause expresses purpose: we bind your tongue in order that we recover fortune.
segments.s637-g75: بگویید is the imperative (pl./polite) 'tell/say'. کز is the classical contracted form (که از) introducing the content clause and here carries the sense 'that/from' (introducing separation from God). 'پاک یزدان خدای' is a noun phrase 'the pure God (Yazdān)', with پاک qualifying یزدان/خدای.
segments.s637-g76: Two coordinated perfective verbs in first-person plural: بریدیم 'we severed/broke off' and بستیم 'we bound/sealed'. 'با دیو رای' is a prepositional phrase 'with the demon's counsel/plan' (با = with, دیو = demon, رای = counsel/plan) and is the complement indicating with whom/with what their counsel is joined.
segments.s637-g77: A conditional preface: اگر 'if' + هرگز 'ever/never' (here classical 'if ever') introduces the condition. 'از رای بهرامشاه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the counsel/advice of Bahram Shah' (از marks separation/source; رای = counsel/opinion; بهرامشاه is the possessor).
segments.s637-g78: بپیچیم is a present/subjunctive 1pl form meaning 'we turn aside/veer away' (ب- prefix + پیچیم), used here under the conditional 'اگر'. و links clauses. داریم is present 'we have/hold' and takes the object 'بد را نگاه' (بد = 'evil/bad' marked by را as definite object, followed by نگاۀ 'gaze/view'); نگاه بد داشتن is an idiom meaning to harbor ill intent or cast an evil eye.
segments.s637-g79: چو is the temporal 'when'. 'سوگند شد' uses شد as a copula/passive marker meaning 'the oath was made/swearing took place'. خورده و ساخته are adjectival/participial modifiers describing the state of the oath ('خورده' = taken/sworn, 'ساخته' = made/arranged) coordinated to emphasize it was performed and finalized; this is a poetic phrasing.
segments.s637-g80: 'دل شاه' is a possessive noun phrase 'the king's heart'. 'زان رنج' is ablative ('from/for that suffering'), and پرداخته is a past participle used predicatively meaning 'has suffered/been affected' (a poetic usage of پرداختن here conveying being made to suffer). The whole means the king's heart was stricken by that grief.
segments.s637-g105: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by که + چون, functioning as 'when'. که here is the conjunction 'that/when' introducing the clause; چون functions as 'when' (classical usage). The noun phrase شاه شنگل is the proper name 'King Shengel' (title + name). سوی جشنگاه places the action directionally: سوی 'toward' + جشنگاه 'place of celebration'.
segments.s637-g106: شُد is a past/habitual/hap axial verb here meaning 'it happened / came to pass' and introduces the result clause. خواستار functions as a noun 'petitioner' (not merely adjectival). آید is the verb 'came' (present/habitual form used narratively). از نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of the king' where از + نزد yields 'to/into the presence of' شاه.
segments.s637-g107: بگوید is a narrative/reporting verb meaning 'he said' (classical present used for past narration); the provided sense list lacked the direct 'say' sense, so the pronouncement function is implied. که introduces the reported content 'that'. برزوی is a proper name (Barzū). شد is the past/resultive 'became' and modifies دردمند 'in pain/distressed' as a predicate: 'Barzū became distressed.'
segments.s637-g108: This line uses V O S (verb–object–subject) word order typical of poetry. پذیردش is the verb 'accepted' with the enclitic object -ش 'him/his/it'. پوزش is the object 'pardon/apology' that was accepted. شه هوشمند is the subject phrase 'the wise king' (شاه with the epithet هوشمند) appearing after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s637-g109: زن is the noun 'the woman' (subject). این بند is the direct object 'this band/cord/attachment' (بند here taken as a physical 'band' or bond). بنهاد is the past tense verb 'placed/put' (archaic past form of نهادن). با مادرش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on/with her mother' where ش is the possessive clitic 'her'.
segments.s637-g110: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'she heard' (third person). پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. مادر is the subject 'the mother'. از here is a preposition 'from/of' and دخترش is 'her daughter' (دختر + possessive clitic -ش). In context the verb for what the mother does next is implied/continues in the following lines; here the segment sets up 'when the mother heard, then (to) her daughter...'.
segments.s637-g111: همی بود is a continuous/narrative past expression 'it was / it remained so'. تا introduces the temporal limit 'until'. تازه شد is 'became fresh/renewed' (تازه 'fresh/new' + شد 'became'). جشنگاه is the place or occasion of festivity, the thing that becomes renewed or newly prepared.
segments.s637-g112: گرانمایگان is a plural nominative 'the illustrious/esteemed ones/nobles'. برگرفتند is the past plural verb 'they took up' or 'they set out' (برگرفتن used here with راه to mean 'set out upon the road'). راه is the noun 'road/way' and completes the verb phrase برگرفتند راه = 'took the road / set out'.
segments.s637-g41: شود is an optative/third-person verbal form meaning 'let it be' or 'may (they) be'; Persian poetry often places the verb before its subjects, so 'شود شاه و لشکر' = 'let the king and the army be'. 'بدان جایگاه' is a prepositional phrase ('به + آن جایگاه' = 'to/at that place') placed after the subject to mark location.
segments.s637-g42: که introduces a relative clause ('which/where') describing that place. بی‌ره is an archaic/variant form (cf. بی‌راه) used here as part of the clause; نماید is a third-person singular present/subjunctive of 'نمودن' meaning 'to show' or 'to appear'; بران is contracted 'بر آن' ('to/on that') and, together with بیشه راه, yields 'the path to that thicket'. The clause therefore specifies the place by noting the path into the grove.
segments.s637-g43: The conditional starts with اگر 'if'. The sequence رفت خواهی has inverted word order: literally 'go – you will/wish', i.e. 'if you want to go'. بدانجای رو: 'بدان' = 'to that' (به + آن) and رو is an imperative/old-form verb meaning 'go' or 'turn (toward)'; together: 'go to that place.'
segments.s637-g44: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. کهن is an adjective meaning 'old' or 'veteran/seasoned' used as a predicative quality. باش is the present-imperative 'be' giving the exhortation 'be always seasoned'. The following وَ links a wish clause 'سال تو نو' where سال 'year' and تو 'your' and نو 'new' form a short benediction 'may your year be new.'
segments.s637-g45: ز امروز = 'from today' (z poetic = از). بشکیب is an imperative 'be patient' (ب + شکیب). تا نیم روز uses تا as 'until' and نیم روز means 'noon' or 'half-day' — here 'until midday.' The clause gives a temporal instruction starting now and lasting to a point in the day.
segments.s637-g46: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' پیدا شود is a two-word predicate where پیدا 'apparent/visible' and شود 'becomes' combine to mean 'appears' or 'becomes visible.' تاج گیتی فروز is a noun phrase: تاج 'crown', گیتی 'world', فروز functioning as an attributive 'illuminating/bright' — 'the crown that lights the world' (i.e. the rising, glorious crown).
segments.s637-g47: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. از شهر بیرون is the prepositional phrase 'out of the city.' رود is the verb (present/subjunctive form of رفتن) 'goes' — note that in the provided lexicon this token is homonymous with nouns but here functions as the verbal form. شهریار is the subject 'the sovereign/king.'
segments.s637-g48: به رفتن literally 'for/to departure' (به + infinitive رفتن) sets purpose: 'prepare for going.' بیارای here is an imperative or causative form meaning 'make ready/prepare' (the supplied lexicon senses don't match the usage, so sense is left unspecified). وَ links the next imperative: بر ساز کار is an idiomatic phrase 'set the work/machine in order' — بر (on/upon) + ساز (means/device) + کار (work) = 'put the plan into operation.'
segments.s637-g9: This is a topical/old-fashioned word order: the person addressed (سپینود) is fronted with the object-marker را, then the speech verb گفت, and finally the speaker (بهرامشاه). In modern Persian you'd expect به سپینود گفت or بهرامشاه به سپینود گفت; here it reads as “Bahramshah said (to) Sepinood.”
segments.s637-g10: که introduces a quoted/content clause: “that …”. دانم is first-person present/factual (I know / I am sure). The second که introduces the subordinate predication “you are …”; هستی is the second-person present copula/verb ‘you are’, while م and را together form the object 'مرا' (me) and mark it as definite; نیک‌خواه is a noun meaning ‘well-wisher’ (one well-disposed toward someone). The whole clause = “that I know that you are a well-wisher of mine.”
segments.s637-g11: یکی راز = 'a certain secret' (one secret). خواهم + (verb) here expresses intention/future ('I will / I intend to'). همی is an archaic/aspectual particle with an ongoing or emphatic flavour placed near the verbal complex. با تو marks the addressee ('to/with you'), and گفت is the lexical verb 'say/tell'. Together: 'I will tell you a secret.'
segments.s637-g12: چنان کن is an imperative 'do thus / do so'. که introduces a purposive/result clause. ماند is a third-person singular verbal form meaning 'remain' (here optative/jussive sense: 'may/should remain'), سخن = 'the matter/word', در نهفت = 'in hiddenness / concealed'. So: 'Do so that the matter remains hidden.'
segments.s637-g13: همی + رفت + خواهم is an older/periphrastic way to express intent/immediate future — literally 'I will go (I intend to go)'. ز هندوستان = 'from India' (ز = poetic 'from'). The sense is 'I will set out from India.'
segments.s637-g14: تو (you) is the explicit subject. باشی is a subjunctive/imperative-form of 'to be' meaning 'be' or 'let you be' (here a hortative: 'you be/you should be'). بدین کار = 'in/with this affair' (بدین is the fused prepositional form 'in/with this'), and همداستان is a predicate adjective/noun 'like‑minded / accomplice'. Together: 'You be of one mind in this affair.'
segments.s637-g15: به تنها = 'in private / alone' (an adverbial phrase). بگویم is the first-person present/subjunctive used for a future/intentional sense 'I will tell' or 'I shall say'. ت + را together make ترا ('to you' / 'you' as indirect object), and یک سخن = 'one word / one thing'. So: 'I will tell you one thing in private.'
segments.s637-g16: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'it must not / one must not'. که introduces the subordinate clause. داند is a present/subjunctive form 'know' (here potential/obligational: 'should know' or 'come to know'). کس in this negative context functions as 'no one', and از انجمن = 'from the assembly/company'. So: 'No one of the assembly must know (of this).'

sentences.s637-s29: Whoever the Iranians were, they resolved to go with him.
sentences.s637-s30: He came; when he drew near the sea he arrived, and on the road he saw the merchants' loads.
sentences.s637-s31: For there were merchants of Iran, and the brave ones were on sea and on land.
sentences.s637-s32: When the merchants saw Bahram's face, the king bit his lip with his teeth.
sentences.s637-s13: The best woman in the world was the one from whom her husband was always cheerful.
sentences.s637-s14: If my pure soul were to turn away from your pledge, I would be loathing life on your account.
sentences.s637-s15: To him Bahram said, 'Then find a remedy, and do not disclose this secret or speak it to anyone.'
sentences.s637-s16: Sepīnūd said, 'O worthy one of the throne, I will build (it) if Fortune becomes my ally.'

sentences.s637-s66: He roused them and made ships and small boats; he put Sepinud into a boat.
sentences.s637-s67: They reached the shore; when day came, the world's champion, the world's illuminator, appeared.
sentences.s637-s41: Then the king said to them thus: "Keep this warning of mine with you."

sentences.s637-s44: There will remain here neither merchants nor king, neither landowners nor army, nor throne and crown.
sentences.s637-s1: When Bahram was joined with the maiden Shangal, his wife recognized him as king of the world.
sentences.s637-s2: Day and night he wept sorely for his love of her; he had set two eyes (i.e. two tearful eyes) in that face of hers.
sentences.s637-s3: When Shangal became aware of their love, his bad suspicions were cut short.
sentences.s637-s4: They sat together one joyful day; they conversed about every kind of thing, matters great and small.

sentences.s637-s58: She, pleading pardon, said: "O king, do not let your heart be troubled on my account in any way."

sentences.s637-s60: Shangal said to his wife, "May this not be so — that, because someone is sick, the celebration be made only a remembrance."
sentences.s637-s61: From Kannauj the night-roving Shangal departed; he turned a scorching face toward the Indians.
sentences.s637-s62: When the night grew dark, King Bahram said, 'The time to go has come, O good pair.'

sentences.s637-s64: He covered the sleepers and himself sat up — a lariat at the saddle-cloth and a mace in his hand.
sentences.s637-s25: Because of his words Bahram became glad; he did not sleep, remaining in thought until dawn.
sentences.s637-s26: When the sun appeared on the vault of the sky, the dark night closed its weight over the strangers.
sentences.s637-s27: He alighted from the baggage of Bahram Gūr; he urged the onager along, with a hunting tune.
sentences.s637-s28: He said to the woman, 'Play (the) tune and tell no one'; we set off, the two of us turning toward the road.

sentences.s637-s50: He kept going, winding, to his own iwan, having entrusted his body and soul to God.
sentences.s637-s51: Thereupon, when Bahram set out toward the road, he said thus to the woman, “O well-wisher.”
sentences.s637-s52: Be not reluctant, mother; contrive a remedy yourself, for you are such that even uprightness will not make the secret known to you.
sentences.s637-s9: My work for Iran is better than this; the Creator of the world is my helper.
sentences.s637-s10: If you set out, counsel will come to you; a good, wise guide will come to you.

sentences.s637-s18: Which the fortunate Maran possess; they praise the idol‑adorned place.

sentences.s637-s20: At that place there was the Guran's game; in Kannauj, inside the gate, agarwood burned.

sentences.s637-s22: If you wish to go, go to that place; remain always seasoned, and may your years be renewed.
